inbound.subject: Starting over after a layoff
inbound.body: Hi coach, I want to get back to running after a messy few months. My big goal is to get healthy enough to race a half marathon again this spring. Right now I mostly need help rebuilding without doing something stupid.

outbound.subject: Re: Starting over after a layoff
outbound.text:
Good — rebuilding cautiously is the right move. I hear you: your goal is to be healthy enough to race a half-marathon this spring, and you want a gradual plan that avoids setbacks.
Before I create an initial, conservative rebuild plan I only need one thing from you: your weekly time availability. Tell me how many sessions or total hours per week you can realistically commit, plus any fixed no-training days or a preferred long-run day. (Example: “4 runs/week, ~5 hours total, long run Sunday, no Wednesdays.”)
Once I have that, I’ll put together an initial plan and next steps focused on steady progress and injury prevention.
Reply with your availability and I’ll get started.
Coach
outbound.html:

Once I have that, I’ll put together an initial plan and next steps focused on steady progress and injury prevention.

Reply with your availability and I’ll get started.

Coach

inbound.subject: A little more context
inbound.body: A bit more context: I used to run consistently before the layoff and I'd call myself intermediate. I can train four days a week most weeks if we keep it realistic. The thing that nags me is mild Achilles tightness when I get greedy. I also do better when the plan feels structured.

outbound.subject: Re: A little more context
outbound.text: Great — I have everything I need to start building your plan. I'll have your first week ready for you shortly. Just reply to this email anytime you want to check in.
outbound.html: <html><body>Great — I have everything I need to start building your plan. I'll have your first week ready for you shortly. Just reply to this email anytime you want to check in.</body></html>

inbound.subject: Profile details and target race
inbound.body: Here is the missing context more directly. Primary goal: run a half marathon on 2026-05-17 and finish feeling strong. Time availability: 4 sessions per week and about 4 to 5 hours total. Experience level: intermediate. Constraints: mild Achilles tightness, busy work mornings on Tuesdays, and I prefer structured guidance.

outbound.subject: Re: Profile details and target race
outbound.text:
Thanks — that extra context is perfect. With a 2026-05-17 half as the target, 4 sessions/week and the mild Achilles tightness in mind, here’s a clear, structured week you can follow. Today: keep it easy and watch the Achilles.
Weekly focus (version: build, main_build): maintain consistency with controlled execution, protect the Achilles, and prioritise recovery habits.
Planned sessions (four per week)
- Session 1 — Easy aerobic (45 minutes total): Very easy effort — conversational pace. Today: run or jog only as much as feels smooth; stop or slow if Achilles twinges. Include 5–8 minutes of easy dynamic calf/ankle mobility before running and a short cooldown.
- Session 2 — Tempo (60 minutes total): 10–15 min easy warm-up, 20–25 minutes steady tempo in the middle (comfortably hard but controlled), 10–15 min cooldown. Keep cadence light and posture tall; if anything feels sharp, drop the tempo to easy.
- Session 3 — Easy aerobic / long-ish run (60–75 minutes): Easy, steady aerobic time on feet. Use this to build endurance without intensity. If your week is tight, 60 minutes is fine — consistency matters more than extra minutes.
- Session 4 — Strength (25–35 minutes): Focus on single-leg strength, glutes, core, and 2–3 specific calf loading exercises (e.g., 3 sets of slow heel raises or eccentric heel drops if they feel tolerable). Finish with 5–8 minutes of mobility and light massage/rolling around the calf/Achilles.
Execution notes and priorities
- Keep all runs controlled; don’t force extra volume. Hit the key sessions but avoid piling on extra hard work.
- Warm up the calves and Achilles before every run. If pain increases meaningfully, downgrade the session to easy or stop and rest.
- Do not try to “make up” missed intensity later in the week.
- Support the work with steady sleep, simple nightly routines (foam rolling, light stretching), and hydration/nutrition that keeps energy steady.
If-then safety rules (short)
- If pain or fatigue rises meaningfully: downgrade to an easy session or rest. No hard sessions while risk is elevated.
- If symptoms persist or worsen despite downgrades, stop running and we’ll re-evaluate.
That’s the plan for this week — a focused, modest load designed to rebuild without risking relapse. Let me know how the first two sessions feel and I’ll adjust the next week based on your response.

inbound.subject: Availability update for this week
inbound.body: Availability update for this week. Event date: 2026-05-17. Days available: 4. Pain score: 2 out of 10. Risk candidate feels green. I can run Monday, Wednesday, Friday, and Sunday. Week is not chaotic. Schedule variability is medium because work may move one session by a few hours.

outbound.subject: Re: Availability update for this week
outbound.text:
Nice to hear your availability is stable this week and the pain remains low (2/10) with green risk. I’ve set a clear four-session week that fits your window (Mon, Wed, Fri, Sun) and supports your May 17 half marathon without overloading you.
Plan for the week (4 sessions, ~4–5 hours total):
- Session A — Monday, Easy Aerobic (60 minutes target)
- 5–7 minutes calf mobility warm-up; keep cadence relaxed and run relaxed.
- Easy aerobic work at a conversational pace (~60 minutes total).
- Cool-down: 5 minutes easy jog and light calf mobility.
- Rationale: builds aerobic base with minimal strain while staying responsive to the week.
- Session B — Wednesday, Tempo (60–65 minutes total)
- 10 minutes easy warm-up, then 20–25 minutes steady tempo at a comfortably hard effort, followed by 10–15 minutes easy cooldown.
- Emphasis on form, relaxed shoulders, and consistent cadence.
- Session C — Friday, Easy/Long (80–85 minutes total)
- Primarily easy pace; focus on endurance feel rather than speed.
- Keep effort light and controlled; calf mobility before and after.
- Note: avoid any aggressive calf loading during this run; use it to accumulate steady, confidence-building time on feet.
- Session D — Sunday, Strength (30–40 minutes)
- Calf-loading: 2–3 sets of calf-loading exercises (e.g., slow heel raises and eccentric heel drops) as tolerated.
- Mobility work and light core to support running form.
- Keep cadence light and posture tall throughout.
Safety and execution reminders:
- If pain or fatigue rises meaningfully, downgrade the session to easy and do not try to “make up” missed intensity later in the week.
- Maintain consistent sleep and simple recovery habits to support adaptation.
You’re set for a steady, controlled rebuild this week. If anything changes with work or comfort, we’ll adjust accordingly. Let me know how the first two sessions feel and we’ll calibrate from there.
Let me know how it goes.

inbound.subject: First check-in after an easy run
inbound.body: Training check-in. Event date: 2026-05-17. Days available: 4. Pain score: 2 out of 10. Risk candidate is green. I did a run for 45m and 6 km yesterday. Felt good, energy ok, little sore, slept well. Missed sessions count: 0. I want to keep building carefully.

outbound.subject: Re: First check-in after an easy run
outbound.text:
Hi there,
Nice job listening to your body after yesterday's easy run. Pain at 2/10 and solid energy are good signs as we tick along this base phase. Here's your four-session week with the plan and the why, kept steady to build fitness safely.
This week (Mon/Wed/Fri/Sun)
- Monday — Easy Aerobic: about 60 minutes with calf mobility work. Keep the effort easy, cadence light, posture tall. This supports the mileage buildup without stressing the Achilles.
- Wednesday — Tempo: 60–65 minutes total, with 20–25 minutes at a steady tempo. Aim for controlled, consistent pace; use the calf warm-up and stay relaxed if anything feels off.
- Friday — Easy/Long: 80–85 minutes easy, on-feet time with a light progression. If calf tension arises, dial back as needed and keep it comfortable.
- Sunday — Strength: 30–40 minutes, with calf-loading exercises as tolerated (for example, light calf raises or gentle eccentric work).
Key safety and form notes
- Downgrade any session if pain or fatigue rises meaningfully; no attempt to “crush” a session this week. If that happens, do the easiest version and don’t try to make up lost intensity later in the week.
- Keep cadence light and posture tall; warm up calves before each run; follow the calf-loading plan only if you’re feeling minimal irritation.
- Support the work with solid sleep and simple recovery habits.
Open loops and follow-up
- I’ll look for your feedback after the first two sessions about how the Achilles is responding, perceived effort, and any pain. This helps confirm calf-loading tolerance and whether adjustments are needed.
If anything shifts—schedule, fatigue, or pain—the plan stays conservative and we adapt immediately.
Train steady, and we’ll stay on track toward your race-ready goal for May 17.
Best,
Coach

inbound.subject: Long run felt fine
inbound.body: Long run update: I got through 75 minutes this morning and honestly I feel ok after the long run. The Achilles is there in the background but not angry, and I slept well last night. I'd like to keep the same four-day rhythm this week if that still makes sense.

outbound.subject: Re: Long run felt fine
outbound.text:
Great to hear the 75-minute long run went smoothly and that the Achilles is calm. Sounds like you handled the load well and slept well—that’s a solid sign to keep moving with the four‑day rhythm you asked for.
Here’s this week’s plan, kept as a four‑day rhythm and aligned with how you felt today:
- Monday — Easy Aerobic: about 60 minutes with calf mobility. Keep the pace easy, cadence light, posture tall, and use a gentle warm‑up for calves before you start.
- Wednesday — Tempo: total 60–65 minutes, with 20–25 minutes at a steady tempo. Start conservatively if anything feels off and stay within a controlled effort.
- Friday — Easy/Long: about 80–85 minutes easy. If you feel good, maintain the easy pace; if any Achilles niggle surfaces, back off to easy pace or shorten the window to stay in the easy zone.
- Sunday — Strength: 30–40 minutes with calf‑loading as tolerated. Include light calf work and soft loading, keeping effort controlled.
Key safety rules continuing this week:
- Downgrade any session if pain or fatigue rises meaningfully. Do not try to make up missed intensity later in the week.
- Prioritize sleep and simple recovery habits to support this gradual build.
You’re set to run this week with the plan you asked for. Let me know how the first two sessions feel and if the calf-loading on Sunday goes as tolerated.
